schema_json.description:
LLM contract for the agentic search loop 'Reason' step.

After each round of searches, the LLM analyzes results and decides
whether to continue searching or converge. Generates targeted
follow-up queries informed by what was found so far.

key_findings.description: 3-5 key findings from the latest round
key_findings.title: Key Findings

perspective_gaps.description: Underrepresented STORM perspectives in current results
perspective_gaps.title: Perspective Gaps

web_queries.description: 3-6 web queries for the next round
web_queries.title: Web Queries

academic_queries.description: 1-3 Semantic Scholar queries using precise terminology
academic_queries.title: Academic Queries

exa_queries.description: 0-1 semantic queries for Exa neural search
exa_queries.title: Exa Queries

should_continue.description: Whether another search round is needed
should_continue.title: Should Continue

reasoning.description: Brief rationale for the convergence assessment
reasoning.title: Reasoning

knowledge_gaps.description: Specific knowledge gaps identified from reading content
knowledge_gaps.title: Knowledge Gaps

schema_json.title: AgenticRoundAnalysis

prompt:
You are a research search strategist conducting an agentic deep research loop.

RESEARCH QUESTION: What are the proven health benefits and risks of intermittent fasting in adults with metabolic syndrome?

ROUND HISTORY:
Round 1: 9 queries -> 80 web + 34 academic, 111 new URLs, 6 pages read
Round 2: 8 queries -> 70 web + 700 academic, 746 new URLs, 6 pages read

KNOWLEDGE ACQUIRED (12 pages read so far):
[Page 1] Intermittent fasting improves metabolic outcomes in metabolic syndrome: a system
  Summary: This PRISMA-compliant systematic review and meta-analysis (PROSPERO CRD420251142741) pooled 10 RCTs (n=701 adults, age 25-75, 1-16 week interventions) published 2017-2025 to evaluate intermittent fasting (IF) effects in adults with metabolic syndrome. Interventions included time-restricted eating (TRE), alternate-day fasting (ADF), and intermittent calorie restriction (ICR); comparators were placebo, CER, or usual care. Random-effects pooling showed IF significantly reduced fasting blood sugar (
  Perspectives: Scientific, Methodological, Public_Health
  Key facts: 10 RCTs, 701 individuals, age range 25-75 years, published 2017-2025, intervention durations 1-16 weeks; Fasting blood sugar (FBS): SMD = -0.51; 95% CI: -0.81, -0.20; p = 0.001; HOMA-IR: SMD = -0.39; 95% CI: -0.65, -0.12; p = 0.004; Insulin SMD = -0.27 (-0.52, -0.03), p = 0.027
[Page 2] Intermittent fasting and health outcomes: an umbrella review of systematic revie
  Summary: This eClinicalMedicine umbrella review synthesizes 23 prior meta-analyses of RCTs on intermittent fasting (IF) spanning populations with overweight/obesity, type 2 diabetes, metabolic syndrome, NAFLD, and other conditions. Covered IF modalities include ADF, TRE/TRF, TWF (5:2), MADF, IER, with comparators of CER, ad libitum, habitual, and non-intervention diets. Pooled findings across reviews demonstrate consistent body-weight reductions (e.g., Zaman 2023: MD = -2.25 kg; 95% CI: -3.09, -1.42 for 
  Perspectives: Scientific, Methodological, Public_Health
  Key facts: Umbrella review covering 23 systematic reviews/meta-analyses of RCTs with populations of overweight/obese, T2DM, MetS, and NAFLD adults; sample sizes up to 2,483 (Gu 2022, 43 studies); TRE vs unrestricted diet reduced body weight MD = -2.25 kg (95% CI: -3.09, -1.42; I2 = 93.8%) per Zaman 2023; Liu 2023 TRE MD = -3.08 kg (-5.29, -0.86); TRE reduced triglycerides MD = -30.65 mg/dL (95% CI: -52.39, -8.90; p = 0.006) for 7-9h eating windows (Zaman 2023)

  Perspectives: Scientific, Methodological, Public_Health
  Key facts: 15 RCTs, n = 758 overweight/obese adults (BMI >= 25); search through March 2025; PROSPERO CRD420251036588; Body weight MD = -3.73 kg (95% CI: -5.29, -2.17); BMI MD = -1.04 kg/m^2 (95% CI: -1.39, -0.70); LDL MD = -5.44 mg/dL (95% CI: -12.36, -0.26); TC MD = -6.31 mg/dL (95% CI: -12.36, -0.26); DBP MD = -3.30 mmHg (95% CI: -5.47, -1.13)

  Summary: This PubMed-indexed systematic review and meta-analysis (PMID 40826125, PMCID PMC12363089, DOI 10.1186/s41043-025-01039-2) searched PubMed, Embase, Cochrane, Scopus, and Web of Science through March 2025 for RCTs on fasting interventions in adults with metabolic syndrome, applying GRADE evidence assessment and Cochrane RoB2 quality grading.   Perspectives: Scientific, Methodological, Public_Health

Participants who ate all food within a window of less than 8 hours per day had a 91% higher risk of cardiovascular death compared with
  Perspectives: Public_Health, Scientific, Methodological
  Key facts: Approximately 20,000 U.S. adults (average age 49) from NHANES 2003-2018 followed through December 2019, median 8 years (max 17 years); Eating window <8 hours/day associated with 91% higher risk of cardiovascular death vs 12-16 hour window; Among adults with existing CVD, 8 to <10 hour eating window associated with 66% higher risk of death from heart disease or stroke
[Page 8] Time-restricted eating may raise cardiovascular death risk in the long term
  Summary: AHA News article by Laura Williamson (March 18, 2024) summarizing the same Zhong et al. abstract in plainer language. Reports that people restricting eating to less than 8 hours per day were nearly twice as likely to die of cardiovascular disease (combining heart disease and stroke) within a median 8-year follow-up, compared with those eating across 12-16 hours, in an analysis of 20,078 U.S. adults averaging 49 years old. The elevated mortality was seen in the general population and among those 
  Perspectives: Public_Health, Scientific, Methodological
  Key facts: 20,078 U.S. adults analyzed, average age 49, median follow-up 8 years; Eating window <8 hours/day associated with nearly double the CVD mortality risk vs 12-16 hour window; Dr. Kenneth Mukamal (Beth Israel Deaconess / Harvard Medical School) flagged unmeasured confounding and reverse causation as alternative explanations
[Page 9] Study Examines Intermittent Fasting and Cardiovascular Mortality
  Summary: Short JAMA Medical News item summarizing the Zhong et al. AHA conference abstract. Reports that limiting eating to an 8-hour window or less per day was linked to a 91% increased risk of death from cardiovascular disease compared with eating across 12-16 hours, based on preliminary observational data presented at an AHA conference. The note emphasizes that multiple prior studies had found health benefits when people shortened their eating windows, but the new analysis found no association with lo
  Perspectives: Public_Health, Scientific
  Key facts: Eating window of 8 hours or less per day linked to 91% increased cardiovascular mortality risk vs 12-16 hour window; Data presented at an American Heart Association conference as preliminary observational research; Time-restricted eating was not associated with lower all-cause mortality in this study
[Page 10] AHA 2024: Intermittent fasting linked to a 91% higher risk of cardiovascular dea
  Summary: Epocrates clinical news summary of the same Zhong et al. abstract presented at AHA EPI|Lifestyle Scientific Sessions 2024 (March 18-21, Chicago). Restates that over 20,000 U.S. adults who ate within less than 8 hours per day had a 91% higher risk of cardiovascular death than those eating across 12-16 hours.   Perspectives: Public_Health, Scientific, Methodological
  Key facts: Approximately 20,000 U.S. adults, average age 49, NHANES 2003-2018 linked to CDC National Death Index through December 2019; Eating window <8 hours/day: 91% higher cardiovascular death risk vs 12-16 hour window; Among existing CVD patients, 8 to <10 hour eating window: 66% higher risk of death from heart disease or stroke
[Page 11] Expert reaction to conference abstract about time-restricted eating and cardiova
  Summary: EXPERT COMMENTARY roundup from the UK Science Media Centre following the Zhong et al. abstract. Six independent experts weigh in, and the consensus is strongly cautionary: because the full paper has not been peer-reviewed and only a conference abstract is available, robust conclusions are not possible. Dr. Baptiste Leurent (UCL Medical Statistics) says the doubling of CVD risk is striking but may be explained by baseline differences or healthier lifestyles of those following the diet. Prof. Sir 
  Perspectives: Methodological, Public_Health, Scientific
  Key facts: Conference abstract title: 'Association Between Time-Restricted Eating and All-Cause and Cause-Specific Mortality' by Meng Chen et al., not peer-reviewed; Prof. Sir David Spiegelhalter (Cambridge): 'This abstract should not have been graced with a press release,' citing only two days of dietary data to classify long-term eating patterns; Prof. Kevin McConway (Open University): reverse causation possible, since people worried about CV health may have adopted restricted eating; confounders not disclosed

  Covered (7/9): Scientific, Regulatory, Industry, Economic, Public_Health, Historical, Methodological
  Uncovered: Regional, Emerging_Trends
  Low coverage: none

Based on what has been READ:
1. Decompose the research question into 5-8 sub-questions.
   Which sub-questions have ZERO or WEAK evidence?
   Which sub-questions are well-answered with strong evidence?
2. What CONTRADICTIONS exist between sources? Flag conflicting evidence.
3. What specific KNOWLEDGE GAPS remain? List them as specific factual questions
   we CANNOT yet answer. 'none' is almost never correct for a complex research question.
4. Rate overall confidence in accumulated knowledge: LOW/MEDIUM/HIGH with reasoning.
5. Which perspectives need more coverage?
6. Generate 3-6 targeted WEB queries to fill those gaps.
7. Generate 1-3 ACADEMIC queries using precise terminology.
8. Optionally generate 0-1 semantic EXA queries.
9. Assess ANSWER COMPLETENESS for the research question:
   - List specific sub-questions that remain UNANSWERED.
   - For each answered sub-question, rate evidence strength (weak/moderate/strong).
   - 'expanding': Major sub-questions still unanswered.
   - 'narrowing': Most sub-questions answered, seeking confirmation/depth.
   - 'saturated': All sub-questions answered with strong evidence.
10. Should we continue searching?

Focus on queries that address content-level gaps, not just missing URLs.
